children.title: You said What is approx admission rate for Harvard gse waitlist

children.value: What is approx admission rate for Harvard gse waitlist

The Harvard Graduate School of Education (HGSE) does not publicly disclose a specific, fixed admission rate for its waitlist because the number of students admitted fluctuates significantly based on the "yield" (the percentage of admitted students who choose to enroll).
However, historical data and general Harvard graduate admissions patterns provide a reliable range for what you can expect.
HGSE generally experiences a high yield, meaning most students who are accepted choose to attend.
This typically leaves very few openings for the waitlist. According to university disclosures and historical trends:

children.description: View source details for citation from The Koppelman Group. Opens side panel.

children.value: In other years, if enrollment targets are not met, the school may admit a significant number (occasionally exceeding 100 across various programs).

children.description: View source details for citation from IvyWise. Opens side panel.

children.value: Unlike undergraduate admissions, which occasionally release waitlist totals (often ranging from 0% to 3% acceptance), graduate programs like HGSE are more opaque. Admission is usually program-specific; being on the waitlist for a specialized Ed.M. program depends entirely on how many people in that specific cohort decline their offer.
For the Class of 2029 across Harvard's broader graduate and undergraduate divisions, the university reported admitting approximately
from the waitlist, though this is a university-wide figure and not specific to the School of Education.

children.description: View source details for citation from The Koppelman Group. Opens side panel.

children.value: High yield (common at Harvard) means fewer waitlist spots.
Smaller, specialized cohorts have less movement than larger master’s programs.
Economic shifts or changes in financial aid packages can cause more students to decline, opening spots.
